motion: This House Believes remote work is better than office work

turns.content: Look, once you actually work remote for a while, it is hard to argue going back makes sense. You cut commuting, you get your time back, and honestly you can focus way better without constant interruptions. People act like productivity drops, but for most roles it actually goes up. And on top of that, people are just less burned out. That matters.

I get the appeal, I really do, but I think people overcorrect on this. Offices are not just about work output, they are about collaboration, culture, and alignment. You lose a lot of that when everything is remote. Conversations become scheduled instead of natural. That slows things down in ways people do not always notice.

But most of that so called collaboration is honestly just noise. Meetings that could be emails, interruptions that break your flow. Remote forces people to be more intentional. And if you need to collaborate, you still can. It just is not constant and draining.

I think you are underestimating how much gets lost. Quick problem solving, mentorship, even just learning by being around experienced people. Those things do not translate cleanly online. Especially for newer employees, remote can feel isolating and slower for growth.
At the end of the day, work should fit into life, not the other way around. Remote gives people flexibility without killing productivity. The trade is worth it for most people.
Flexibility is great, but not if it weakens how teams function long term. Offices still solve real problems that remote has not fully replaced.

motion: This House Would ban smartphones for children under 14

turns.content: I think banning them outright is too extreme. Yeah, there are issues, but smartphones are also how kids communicate, learn, and stay connected. The problem is not the device, it is how it is used. Parents should manage that, not a blanket ban.

I do not think it is extreme when you look at what is happening. Attention issues, social media pressure, exposure to things they are not ready for. These are not small problems. A clear boundary like under 14 gives kids time to develop without all that noise.
But kids are still going to find ways around it, or feel left out socially. And not all usage is bad. There are educational tools, communication with family, safety reasons. A ban ignores the nuance.

Sure, no policy is perfect, but doing nothing is worse. You can still allow limited devices or controlled access, but smartphones specifically are too much, too early. Sometimes simple rules work better than hoping everyone manages it well.
